segments.title: The 1801 precedent at Copenhagen
segments.summary: The opening recap establishes the 1801 attack on Copenhagen and the Danish fleet as the direct precedent for Britain's return six years later.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with the first Battle of Copenhagen, naming Admiral Lord Parker and Lord Nelson as his second in command. The concise recap centers on what happened to Danish naval power at the capital: ships were destroyed, captured, or taken away. That outcome supplies the direct precedent for the 1807 expedition rather than merely serving as background from an earlier war. Opening the video here helps a viewer understand the second attack as repeated British coercion against the same fleet and capital within a single decade.

evidence.note: The transcript recaps Parker, Nelson, and the capture of the Danish fleet at Copenhagen.

segments.title: Britain returns for the Danish fleet in 1807
segments.summary: Admiral James Gambier's 1807 expedition combines Royal Navy and army power to isolate Copenhagen and take the Danish fleet again.
segments.body: Six years after the first attack, the Royal Navy returns under Admiral James Gambier with an army in support. Arthur Wellesley appears in the expedition and fights Danish troops away from the capital, showing that the operation was not confined to a fleet action in Copenhagen's waters. Dr. Clarke presents the renewed assault as an almost complete rerun: British forces close on Copenhagen and the Royal Navy takes the Danish fleet again. This watch point is useful for seeing how naval coercion depended on land forces to contain opposition and secure the strategic prize.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Gambier's fleet, accompanying army forces, Wellesley, and the second capture of the Danish fleet.

segments.title: Why Britain repeated the Copenhagen attack
segments.summary: The closing comparison links both Copenhagen attacks to Britain's determination to prevent Danish naval power from joining France.
segments.body: The video closes by placing the 1801 and 1807 attacks side by side: twice within less than a decade, the Royal Navy came to the Danish capital and took its fleet. Dr. Clarke compresses the British strategic rationale into a blunt warning that Denmark should not join France. The comparison makes control of a potentially hostile fleet the connecting issue, rather than treating Copenhagen as a one-time target. This is a concise watch point on how alliance politics and fear of enemy access to naval forces could drive coercive action against a formally separate power.

evidence.note: The transcript compares 1801 and 1807 and states that Denmark should not join the French.
